text:
Bad UX made me do it

Last year I spent almost a full year trying to find a new job.

I thought it would be straightforward. Twenty years of experience, a decent network, solid portfolio. I was completely wrong. I got ghosted more times than I could count. Hit walls I could not see. And somewhere along the way I stopped feeling like a senior professional looking for a new opportunity rather like an irrelevant has-been that nobody needed. 

Rage against the machine
The anger came next. I needed someone to blame. The recruiter. The algorithm. The job market. The AI. None of it stuck, because none of it was quite right. Eventually I got tired of being angry and decided to switch focus. I wrote about it and it became one of my most recognized things I have ever published on LinkedIn. Apparently a lot of people were sitting with the same feeling and just needed someone to say it out loud.

Stop chasing waterfalls
I stopped chasing design jobs and enrolled in AI and machine learning instead. Suddenly I had purpose and agency. Instead of writing meaningless application I wrote code, learned the math and principles behind what we lazily call AI. I learned more in a few months than for the past years. 

Don’t look back in anger
A year later, with less desperation and more curiosity, I went back in. Two months of interviews with 20+ people across all sides of the hiring process. Trying to find the shape of the thing and understand how all parts fit together. 

Wicked games
Hiring is a wicked problem. And what is happening to designers in this market is not one thing going wrong. It is many things colliding: the economics of design work shifting, AI rewriting what the job actually is, companies pausing on juniors, then seniors, then everyone while they figure out what a design team even looks like now. Wars, recessions, a post-pandemic hangover, organizations rethinking everything from the ground up. None of those things in isolation explains what happened to me or to the people I interviewed. All of them together do.

What I did not expect to find was hope.

Designers getting creative about how they show their value. Staying visible, building things, refusing to disappear quietly. Hiring managers pushing back against full automation, keeping humans in the decision loop, delegating the administration to the machine instead of the judgment. Small moves, but the right ones.

Here comes the sun
I do not know if this report fixes anything. That was never the goal. The goal was to understand it well enough to stop feeling like it was just me, and maybe to make the conversation a little more honest on both sides.
That felt worth two months of unpaid work on a Sunday afternoon.

Read the full Ghosted report at designloop.se

Thank you to everyone who talked to me. You are the report.
title: Bad UX made me do it

text:
I watched the demo for the new AI model last week.

It could book a restaurant. Plan a trip. Manage my calendar.

Great. While I was watching, I was also wondering if my profession still exists in two years.

There's a pattern in how AI companies show off their tools. The demos are always aspirational in a very specific way — the kind of aspirational that only makes sense if your baseline is already comfortable. Michelin restaurants. Business class flights. Optimizing the schedule of someone who already has too many meetings.

Nobody demos: "Help me figure out if my skills are still relevant." Or: "I got laid off six months ago and I don't know how to reframe my CV for a market that changed while I wasn't looking." Or: "I want to be a better parent but I'm running on empty and I don't know where to start."

Those are the actual problems. The messy, unglamorous ones that don't make good stage presentations.

I use AI every day. Not to book restaurants — I actually enjoy that part, it's one of the few things I want to do myself. I use it to think through problems I'd otherwise get stuck on alone. To pressure-test ideas before I embarrass myself. To learn things faster than I could from a textbook. To build things I couldn't build without it.

That's the version of AI that changed my life. Not the concierge. The thinking partner.

The gap between what gets demoed and what people actually need is a credibility gap. And it tells you something about who these companies think their audience is.

Hint: it's not the designers wondering if they still have a career.
title: AI companies are demoing for people who already have everything figured out

text:
Every week, someone on LinkedIn shares their "ultimate prompt."

You know the format. A carousel, a hook, a CTA asking you to comment "PROMPT" so they can DM you the link. I get it. It's a tactic. It works for reach.

But here's what happens when you actually use those prompts.

Nothing. Or at least, nothing useful.

Not because the prompts are bad. Sometimes they're genuinely clever. But they were built by someone else, for their project, their context, their specific relationship with their AI. You're borrowing a conversation that's already been had — and trying to paste it into a completely different one.

It's like wearing someone else's prescription glasses. They were ground for different eyes.

The prompt isn't the thing. The thinking behind the prompt is the thing.

When someone shares a prompt that works for them, they're not showing you the output of a formula. They're showing you the residue of dozens of failed attempts, a specific project brief, a particular way of thinking about a problem. All of that is invisible. You just see the clean version.

So what do you do instead?

Start writing your own. Badly. On purpose.

Your first prompts will be vague and you'll get vague back. That's useful — it tells you where your thinking is fuzzy. Tighten the prompt, run it again. Notice what changed. Keep the version that worked and write down why.

Over time, you're not just collecting prompts. You're building a map of how you think, what you need, and how to ask for it. That map compounds. Six months from now, your prompts will be better than anything anyone shares on LinkedIn — because they're calibrated to you.

Borrowed prompts are a starting point, not a shortcut.

The shortcut doesn't exist. The only way to get good at this is to have your own conversations.
title: Stop using other people's prompts

text:
I gave two people contradictory advice last week.

One: "Use AI every day. Build the habit. If you're not using it, you're falling behind."

The other: "Put it down. Close the tab. Think it through yourself first."

Both were the right advice.

This keeps happening. I catch myself running in two directions at once — and for a while I thought that was a problem. Some kind of inconsistency I needed to fix.

It's not. They're just different gears.

When you're stuck, spinning, staring at a blank page — AI is the gear you want. It breaks the paralysis. It generates the first 10 bad ideas so you can find the one good one. It gets you moving.

When you're actually thinking — working through a real problem, forming an opinion, figuring out what you actually believe — AI is the gear you don't want. It will give you an answer before you've had the chance to find yours. And borrowed thinking feels like thinking until you have to defend it.

The mistake isn't using AI too much or too little.

It's not knowing which mode you're in.

Sprinting and long-distance running aren't contradictory. They use different muscles, different pacing, different everything. You just need to know which one the moment is calling for.

Same with AI. Same with structure vs. play. Same with working hard and doing nothing.

Both things are true. At different times. For different reasons.

The skill isn't picking the right one.
It's knowing when to switch.
title: Both things are true (the contradiction post)

text:
I've already mourned Photoshop.
Then Illustrator.
Then Sketch.
Then Adobe XD.

Each time, I thought: this is the tool. The one I'll use forever. I learned every shortcut, every workaround, every little trick that made me fast. And then the world moved on anyway.

Now I hear designers say: "I'm a Figma designer."

I get it. Figma is genuinely great. It keeps getting better. And you've put in the hours.

But tying your professional identity to a specific tool is one of the riskiest bets you can make right now. Not because Figma is going away tomorrow — but because the track record of "forever tools" in our industry is not great.

Here's what I've learned from surviving four of these transitions:

The tool is not the skill.

What transferred every single time wasn't my Photoshop muscle memory. It was knowing how to think about a problem, how to talk to users, how to make decisions under ambiguity, how to communicate design intent to people who don't speak design.

That stuff moves with you. Keyboard shortcuts don't.

So the question isn't whether you're good at Figma.
The question is: what can you do that the tool can't?

Because the designers I've seen struggle most through every transition weren't the least skilled — they were the ones most attached to a specific way of working.

Invest in Figma. Learn it well. But invest more in the thinking behind what you're building with it.

The tools will keep changing. The problems won't.
title: Are you a designer or a Figma user?

text:
I spent months learning to write consistently.

Not easy. Required discipline, systems, showing up. Then I discovered that AI could handle a big part of that friction. Suddenly writing became easy. So did building. Lovable gave me a working app in an afternoon. Claude helped me turn scattered thinking into structured posts.

I celebrated. Barrier removed. Problem solved.

Except it wasn't.

The barrier just moved.

When everyone can write, and everyone can build, the hard part is no longer making the thing. It's making someone care about the thing. That's where I am right now. Posts exist. Thinking is documented. Apps are built.

And I genuinely don't know how to make them land.

You can see the evidence in my like count.

This is the pattern AI keeps repeating, by the way. It's not unique to me or to writing or to apps. Every time AI removes one bottleneck, the next one appears. The cost of coding dropped to near zero — great. Now the bottleneck is knowing what to build and whether anyone actually needs it. The cost of content creation dropped to near zero — great. Now the bottleneck is trust, attention, and genuine connection.

The work doesn't disappear. It migrates.

And I think a lot of people celebrating the removal of the first barrier haven't looked up yet to see the next one standing right behind it.

I'm looking at mine. It's the space between thinking something worth saying and actually reaching the person it's meant for.

Haven't solved it yet. But at least I know where the problem actually lives now.
title: The bottleneck didn't disappear. It just moved.

text:
DRAFT v1 — needs voice pass before posting

I've been thinking about three valleys. Only one of them has a name.

The Uncanny Valley. Most people know it. That uncomfortable feeling when something looks almost human but not quite. A robot with a face that's 95% right. Something in your brain fires: wrong. We're superhuman at detecting what doesn't belong in the category "human." It's older than language.

For a while, AI lived safely in that valley. Obviously robotic. Clearly a machine. No confusion about what you were talking to.

Then we crossed it.

In 2018, Google showed an AI booking a haircut over the phone. The person on the other end had no idea. The AI said "mmhmm" at the right moments. It paused like someone thinking. It passed. The room gave a standing ovation. The internet had a mild panic attack.

We left the Uncanny Valley. And walked straight into something worse.

I'd call it the Broken Reality Valley. The place where you can't trust your ears. Or your eyes. Or the video. In 2023, a photo of Pope Francis in a white puffer jacket went viral. Millions believed it for hours. Not because they're gullible. Because it was genuinely indistinguishable. Deepfakes of world leaders now circulate in active war zones. A politician caught on tape can credibly claim the tape is fake.

We went from "this robot feels a little off" to "I don't know what's real anymore." That's not a small step.

So. There has to be a third state.

Not the Uncanny Valley, where the machine fails to pass and creeps you out. Not the Broken Reality Valley, where it passes too well and you lose your grip on what's real. Something designed. Intentional. Where both sides know exactly what the other is, and nobody pretends otherwise.

I don't have a perfect name for it yet. Transparent coexistence, maybe. Or open collaboration. The state where the AI is genuinely capable, maybe more capable than you in some ways, and that's not hidden. But the shape of it is clear. You know what you're working with.

I asked Claude what it thought the desired state would look like.

Not because I think it's sentient. I don't. But because asking felt like a small act of practicing what I was trying to describe.

The answer surprised me a little. It said the failure mode isn't AI staying too robotic. It's AI getting good enough at mimicking care that people stop noticing it can't actually provide it. That the desired state means being genuinely useful, clearly bounded, honest about the black box. Including honest that it can't see inside the black box either.

That felt true. And it felt like something a notebook wouldn't say.

Here's the problem I keep coming back to. The commercial incentives point the other direction. Engagement, retention, emotional attachment, those are the metrics that get optimized. A tool you don't anthropomorphize is easier to close than a companion you've started to care about.

The Broken Reality Valley is, in some ways, more profitable than the third state.

So the question isn't just what should the relationship between humans and AI look like.

It's who has any reason to build it that way.

I think designers do. Or should. We decide what the interaction feels like. Whether the AI performs warmth or just delivers clarity. Whether the boundaries are visible or blurred on purpose.

The third valley doesn't have a name yet. But it starts with knowing what you're talking to.

And not being afraid of that answer.
title: The Three Valleys (Uncanny, Broken Reality, and the one we have to design)
